content.text: The central design decision when building a diffusion language model is the choice of

: do tokens corrupt in a continuous embedding space (Gaussian noise) or in a discrete space (categorical transitions)? This choice affects the training objective, whether log-likelihood can be computed exactly, whether output tokens are guaranteed to be valid vocabulary items, and what kinds of latent-space operations (interpolation, arithmetic) are possible.

attrs.alt: Discrete vs continuous diffusion comparison diagram

content.text: Standard diffusion models (DDPM, Score SDE) are defined over continuous spaces:
. The forward process adds Gaussian noise, the reverse process learns to denoise, and the score function
Text is discrete: a sentence is a sequence of integer token ids
is a vocabulary of size 30k–100k. There is no natural notion of
for token id 4231. Three approaches have been tried:
Embed then diffuse: map tokens to embeddings in ℝᵈ, apply Gaussian diffusion, round back to tokens.
Diffuse in discrete space: define a Markov chain directly on V (absorbing, uniform, or scored transitions).
Diffuse on the simplex: diffuse probability vectors Δ|V| — a hybrid that is continuous but constrained.
Continuous diffusion LMs (e.g., Diffusion-LM by Li et al., 2022) embed each token into a learned embedding
and apply the standard Gaussian forward process:
. At the end of the reverse chain, the final embedding must be
Rounding introduces a gap between the continuous ELBO and the true discrete log-likelihood. The model optimises a bound on
in token space. The two are related by:
This sum is intractable and the gap is not bounded. As a result, BPD numbers from continuous diffusion LMs are not directly comparable to autoregressive models, which compute exact

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F


def continuous_diffusion_loss(
    denoiser,           # predicts z0_hat from zt
    embedder,           # maps token ids to embeddings
    x0: torch.Tensor,  # (B, L) token ids
    t: torch.Tensor,   # (B,) diffusion timesteps
    alphas_bar: torch.Tensor,  # (T+1,) cumulative alpha schedule
) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Simple MSE loss in embedding space for continuous diffusion LM."""
    z0 = embedder(x0)                          # (B, L, d)
    sqrt_ab  = alphas_bar[t].sqrt()[:, None, None]
    sqrt_1ab = (1 - alphas_bar[t]).sqrt()[:, None, None]

    noise = torch.randn_like(z0)
    zt = sqrt_ab * z0 + sqrt_1ab * noise       # (B, L, d)

    z0_hat = denoiser(zt, t)                   # (B, L, d)
    return F.mse_loss(z0_hat, z0)


def round_to_vocab(z_hat: torch.Tensor, embedding_matrix: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Round each embedding in z_hat to the nearest vocabulary token.
    z_hat            : (B, L, d)
    embedding_matrix : (V, d) — the learnable token embeddings
    Returns          : (B, L) token ids
    """
    # Compute cosine or Euclidean distance to every vocab embedding
    # Using Euclidean for simplicity; cosine also common
    # (B, L, V) = (B, L, d) @ (d, V)
    dists = torch.cdist(z_hat, embedding_matrix.unsqueeze(0).expand(z_hat.size(0), -1, -1))
    return dists.argmin(dim=-1)  # (B, L)
during training to encourage embeddings to stay near vocabulary centroids:
is the embedding matrix and the dot-product acts as an unnormalised similarity. Even with this auxiliary loss, a gap remains.
Discrete diffusion defines a Markov chain on token ids directly. The seminal framework is D3PM (Austin et al., 2021), which parameterises the forward process via a
corresponding to the current token — giving the marginal probability of transitioning to each vocabulary item.

attrs.latex: q(x_t \mid x_0) = \text{Cat}(x_t;\; \bar{Q}_t^\top x_0) \quad \text{where } \bar{Q}_t = Q_1 Q_2 \cdots Q_t

content.text: Each token → [MASK] w.p. mₜ, stays w.p. 1-mₜ
Each token → any other w.p. βₜ/(V-1)
Corrupt to similar tokens more often
The absorbing schedule is dominant in practice because: (a) the closed form
is a simple Bernoulli draw, (b) the reverse posterior has an analytic form, and (c) it matches BERT-style masked language modelling, enabling pre-training.
Because the state space is discrete, the ELBO can be computed exactly without a rounding gap. The reverse posterior for the absorbing case is:

attrs.latex: q(x_{t-1} \mid x_t, x_0) = \begin{cases}\delta_{x_{t-1}, x_0} & x_t \neq [\textsc{M}] \\\frac{\bar{m}_{t-1}}{\bar{m}_t}\,\delta_{[\textsc{M}]}+ \left(1-\frac{\bar{m}_{t-1}}{\bar{m}_t}\right)\delta_{x_0}& x_t = [\textsc{M}]\end{cases}

content.text: This gives an ELBO that reduces to cross-entropy at masked positions — directly tractable and comparable to autoregressive BPD numbers (both are bounds on the same

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

MASK_ID = 103   # [MASK] token id
T = 1000


def cosine_mbar(t: torch.Tensor, T: int = T) -> torch.Tensor:
    s = 0.008
    f = lambda u: torch.cos((u / T + s) / (1 + s) * (torch.pi / 2)) ** 2
    return (1 - f(t) / f(torch.zeros_like(t))).clamp(0, 1)


def discrete_diffusion_loss(
    model,              # predicts logits over V for each position
    x0: torch.Tensor,  # (B, L) token ids
) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    ELBO loss for absorbing discrete diffusion.
    Exactly comparable to AR cross-entropy per token.
    """
    B, L = x0.shape
    t = torch.randint(1, T + 1, (B,), device=x0.device)

    mbar_t = cosine_mbar(t)[:, None]               # (B, 1)
    mask   = torch.bernoulli(mbar_t.expand(B, L)).bool()
    xt     = x0.clone()
    xt[mask] = MASK_ID

    logits = model(xt, t)                           # (B, L, V)
    # Loss only at masked positions
    loss = F.cross_entropy(
        logits[mask],   # (N_masked, V)
        x0[mask],       # (N_masked,)
    )
    return loss
Simplex diffusion (Richemond et al., 2022; Stark et al., 2022) operates on the probability simplex
— the set of probability vectors over the vocabulary. Each token is represented as a one-hot vertex of the simplex at
and diffuses toward the centre (uniform distribution) over time.
The forward process uses a Dirichlet-like SDE or an ODE on the simplex:

Key advantage: the state is always a valid probability vector so the argmax gives a valid token without rounding. Key disadvantage: the geometry of the simplex is non-Euclidean, making it harder to train with standard score-matching objectives.
The following shows the key structural difference between a continuous and discrete diffusion LM at inference time, where the trade-offs are most visible:

# ── Continuous diffusion decode ──────────────────────────────────────────────
@torch.no_grad()
def continuous_decode(
    denoiser,
    embedder,
    embedding_matrix: torch.Tensor,   # (V, d)
    seq_len: int = 64,
    steps: int = 100,
    T: int = 1000,
    device: str = "cpu",
) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Reverse DDPM in embedding space, then round."""
    # Start from pure noise
    d = embedding_matrix.size(1)
    zt = torch.randn(1, seq_len, d, device=device)

    alphas_bar = torch.linspace(0.9999, 0.0001, T + 1).to(device)
    timesteps  = torch.linspace(T, 1, steps, dtype=torch.long)

    for t_val in timesteps:
        t_tensor = t_val.expand(1).long()
        z0_hat   = denoiser(zt, t_tensor)            # (1, L, d) predicted clean embedding

        # DDPM reverse step: zt-1 from zt
        ab_t    = alphas_bar[t_val]
        ab_prev = alphas_bar[max(0, t_val - T // steps)]
        mu      = (ab_prev.sqrt() * z0_hat
                   + ((1 - ab_prev) / (1 - ab_t)).sqrt() * (zt - ab_t.sqrt() * z0_hat))
        sigma   = ((1 - ab_prev) * (1 - ab_t / ab_prev) / (1 - ab_t)).sqrt()
        zt      = mu + sigma * torch.randn_like(zt)

    # ⚠ Rounding step — required, but introduces gap
    return round_to_vocab(zt, embedding_matrix)   # (1, L) token ids


# ── Discrete diffusion decode (masked diffusion) ─────────────────────────────
@torch.no_grad()
def discrete_decode(
    model,                             # bidirectional Transformer
    seq_len: int = 64,
    steps: int = 100,
    T: int = 1000,
    device: str = "cpu",
) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Reverse masked diffusion — output is always valid token ids."""
    xt = torch.full((1, seq_len), MASK_ID, dtype=torch.long, device=device)
    timesteps = torch.linspace(T, 1, steps, dtype=torch.long, device=device)

    for i, t_val in enumerate(timesteps):
        t_next  = timesteps[i + 1] if i + 1 < len(timesteps) else torch.tensor(0)
        logits  = model(xt, t_val.expand(1))          # (1, L, V) — always valid vocab
        x0_hat  = logits.argmax(-1)                   # greedy; can sample instead

        mbar_t    = cosine_mbar(t_val.expand(1))
        mbar_next = cosine_mbar(t_next.expand(1))
        stay_prob = (mbar_next / mbar_t.clamp(1e-9)).clamp(0, 1)

        still_masked = xt == MASK_ID
        reveal = still_masked & (torch.rand_like(xt.float()) > stay_prob[:, None])
        xt_new = xt.clone()
        xt_new[reveal] = x0_hat[reveal]
        xt = xt_new

    return xt   # ✓ All values are valid token ids — no rounding needed
Bits-per-dimension (BPD) or bits-per-character (BPC) is the standard comparison metric. The key complication: continuous and discrete models compute
Exact log p(x₀) via chain rule
ELBO on log p(x₀) — tight bound
ELBO on log p(z₀) in embedding space

To compare continuous diffusion LMs to AR models, practitioners often use either: (a) importance-weighted ELBO (IWAE), which tightens the bound with more samples, or (b) MCMC-based likelihood estimation using annealed importance sampling. Neither is exact; the gap has been estimated at 0.1–0.5 BPC for well-trained models.

Use discrete (absorbing) diffusion when you need: valid discrete outputs, fair likelihood comparison, BERT-compatible pre-training, or efficient inference (no rounding pass). This is the current best-practice for text generation (MDLM, MSKD).

Use continuous diffusion when you need: latent-space interpolation (useful for controlled generation, style transfer, or structured prediction), or when working with embeddings rather than raw token ids (e.g. diffusing over sentence embeddings).
Use simplex diffusion for structured categorical outputs where interpolation is meaningful and you need bounded state — for example, predicting token distributions conditioned on latent variables.
If using a continuous diffusion LM, the rounding step quality matters more than the denoiser quality at very small noise levels. Three improvements over argmax rounding:

def soft_round(
    z_hat: torch.Tensor,           # (B, L, d) predicted clean embedding
    embedding_matrix: torch.Tensor,# (V, d)
    temperature: float = 0.1,
) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Soft rounding via temperature-scaled cosine similarity.
    temperature < 1 sharpens toward argmax; temperature > 1 samples more broadly.
    Returns (B, L) token ids.
    """
    # Normalise both for cosine similarity
    z_norm = torch.nn.functional.normalize(z_hat, dim=-1)       # (B, L, d)
    e_norm = torch.nn.functional.normalize(embedding_matrix, dim=-1).t()  # (d, V)

    logits = torch.einsum("bld,dv->blv", z_norm, e_norm) / temperature    # (B, L, V)
    return torch.multinomial(logits.softmax(-1).view(-1, logits.size(-1)), 1).view(
        z_hat.size(0), z_hat.size(1)
    )
Comparing BPD across model classes without accounting for the rounding gap: a continuous diffusion LM at 1.40 BPD is NOT better than an AR model at 1.42 BPD — they measure different things.

Forgetting to add the rounding loss in continuous models: without it, the model optimises purely in embedding space and embeddings drift away from vocabulary centroids, making rounding at inference much worse.

Using gradient-based search through the discrete token space: gradients from the denoiser w.r.t. input tokens are not meaningful because the forward process is not differentiable through the discrete sampling step. Use Gumbel-Softmax or straight-through estimators if you need gradients.

Assuming discrete and continuous diffusion have the same hyperparameter sensitivity: continuous models are very sensitive to the embedding dimension and L2 weight on the auxiliary rounding loss; discrete models are sensitive to the mask rate schedule and timestep weighting.
Continuous diffusion applies Gaussian corruption to token embeddings — well-studied, but requires a rounding step that introduces an unquantified likelihood gap.
Discrete diffusion (absorbing/masking) defines a categorical Markov chain on token ids — outputs are always valid tokens and the ELBO is exact, enabling fair BPD comparison with AR.
Simplex diffusion is a hybrid: it diffuses probability vectors on Δ|V|, avoiding rounding while retaining continuous geometry.
The rounding gap is the key liability of continuous approaches; MDLM and similar discrete models have closed this gap and now dominate on text generation benchmarks.
Choose discrete (absorbing) diffusion for most NLP tasks; continuous diffusion when you specifically need smooth interpolation in embedding space.
